similar_incidents.investigation_scope: Display brightness cannot be adjusted evidence summary for CPL-100-013: DPX-25949-1247 investigation, EVID-C100-013-202313, TC-DISP-013 verification, and software change request, code review checklist, unit-test log, integration test report, system verification result, and traceability review; the record demonstrates that the corrected software path passes requirements-based tests and does not reintroduce the complaint failure during regression for Brightness menu writes were rejected after configuration CRC mismatch in the display preference block.
similar_incidents.root_cause: Brightness menu writes were rejected after configuration CRC mismatch in the display preference block

similar_incidents.investigation_scope: CDM-4156 evidence basis: investigation of "Volume setting potentiometer ADC channel saturated because reference divider solder joint was cracked", review of DPX-23100-2064, verification through TC-ALRM-056, and supplier corrective-action review, incoming inspection update, component lot containment, and verification of revised acceptance criteria. Required records include SCAR or supplier response, lot genealogy, incoming inspection data, functional verification report, and supplier effectiveness check.
similar_incidents.root_cause: Volume setting potentiometer ADC channel saturated because reference divider solder joint was cracked

similar_incidents.investigation_scope: RISK-PWR-010 evidence package for Incorrect battery percentage displayed: TC-PWR-010 result, REQ-PWR-010 traceability review, returned-unit record DPX-22730-1190, evidence file EVID-C100-010-202310, and power-path design review, bench fault injection, battery or charger characterization, firmware log review, and requirements verification; closure is supported when the power-control path recovers, warns, or safely blocks measurement under the root-cause challenge. Root-cause focus: Fuel-gauge sense resistor tolerance at the high end biased remaining-capacity calculation downward.

similar_incidents.investigation_scope: REQ-DISP-012/TC-DISP-012 evidence for Display FPC latch relaxed after drop shock, intermittently opening the SPI clock contact: objective results from EVID-C100-012-202312 plus extended reproducibility testing, diagnostic log review, returned-unit inspection, and complaint trend review. The evidence supports Closed - confirmed device failure because the reported condition is not reproducible after defined stress testing and no systemic signal is present.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-051 demonstrates three consecutive high SpO2 simulations with alarm onset at or below 5 seconds, correct visual and audible priority, an alarm-history timestamp for each run, and no missed annunciation while display refresh and wireless synchronization are active.
requirements.alarm_scenario: High SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review treats this case as a high-priority physiological alarm annunciation check. The test must confirm distinctive audible signal generation, red visual alarm indication, priority consistency, event latching, operator acknowledgement behavior, and recovery when SpO2 returns below the upper limit. Evidence must show the high limit is not evaluated only on the falling edge.
requirements.text: High SpO2 alarm does not trigger.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-052 reproduces the customer sequence, confirms low SpO2 annunciation within 6 seconds after the valid desaturation condition, logs the alarm event with the correct priority, and verifies no mute or inhibit state persists after restart.
requirements.alarm_scenario: Low SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 assessment focuses on a high-priority desaturation alarm. Verification shall challenge the inhibit timer, silence control, visible alarm symbol, audible tone pattern, and alarm reset path. The test evidence must prove a transient sensor-off condition cannot suppress a subsequent true low SpO2 alarm.
requirements.text: Low SpO2 alarm does not trigger.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-053 produces a high pulse condition during active BLE synchronization and records alarm annunciation within 6 seconds, correct event priority, no dropped alarm icon update, and queue latency within the documented budget for all three runs.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is a physiological alarm priority and queue-loading challenge. The alarm signal must remain recognizable and timely while the device is transferring data, and the visual indicator shall match the audible alarm state. Queue depth shall be captured to show that noncritical synchronization cannot mask a high pulse condition.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-054 confirms the low pulse limit survives configuration save and restore, the alarm triggers within 7 seconds for the simulator bradycardia profile, and diagnostic output proves the comparison uses bpm rather than packed BCD data.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review covers correct alarm-limit storage, restoration, and presentation after power cycle. Verification shall confirm the operator-visible low pulse setting matches the internal comparator value, alarm priority is retained, and audible/visual signals are generated for a sustained low pulse event.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-055 applies noisy signal-quality profiles without a physiological limit violation and the unit produces no continuous audible alarm, no misleading visual alarm latch, and a diagnostic log showing the nuisance request was rejected by the alarm-state filter.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 evaluation is centered on alarm reliability and avoidance of alarm fatigue. The review must confirm that audible signals are generated only for valid alarm states, that visual indicators agree with the audible output, and that the operator can identify whether the alarm is physiological or technical.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-056 steps through each allowed volume setting, confirms the displayed setting matches the driver command, verifies audible output at the selected level, and documents that the cracked reference-divider failure mode is detected rather than accepted as a valid volume request.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration includes audibility of alarm signals at the configured setting and protection against silent or unintended output. Verification shall check volume setting range, audible signal operation, user indication of the selected level, and safe behavior when the volume-control input is electrically out of range.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-057 activates alarm silence, verifies automatic re-annunciation after the configured interval, power-cycles the unit, and confirms the alarm state returns enabled with an alarm-history record for the failed or corrected latch write.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review addresses alarm-silence controls and prevention of indefinite alarm suppression. The test must confirm the silence period is visible to the operator, audible and visual alarms resume after expiry, and a power cycle cannot preserve an unsafe mute latch.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-058 runs the combined waveform, logging, and BLE load profile and each alarm annunciates within the specified latency limit, with measured onset times, queue depth, scheduler trace, and no missed visual indicator across the repeated test sequence.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is alarm-system response time under worst-case device load. Verification must include simultaneous visual and audible alarm generation, display update timing, event logging, and confirmation that non-alarm tasks cannot delay a required physiological alarm beyond specification.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-059 triggers each alarm state used in the complaint, confirms the expected icon appears on the active measurement page, verifies the icon clears only after the alarm rule is satisfied, and captures screenshots plus display-task logs for the corrected asset pointer.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review covers visual alarm signal presence and consistency with audible annunciation. The test must confirm icon color, location, persistence, and synchronization with the audible alarm state after theme or display-page updates.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-060 drives three onset-clear cycles, confirms alarm continuation only during the specified debounce interval, verifies tone and icon clearing after recovery, and records no stale alarm latch or extra alarm-history entry after the condition is cleared.
requirements.alarm_scenario: Alarm continues after condition is cleared.
requirements.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is alarm reset behavior and prevention of confusing stale alarm signals. Verification shall check audible cessation, visual indicator clearing, event-history closeout, and correct response to repeated alarm onset and recovery transitions.
requirements.text: Alarm continues after condition is cleared.

Pass when TC-DISP-011 demonstrates five cold starts and five warm starts on returned-equivalent hardware with a visible first frame within the startup budget, no blank display after panel reset, diagnostic logging for simulated flex-clock failure, and successful recovery without loss of alarm or measurement status.
requirements.regulatory_note: Usability review determined that a blank screen prevents the operator from confirming readiness, battery state, alarm status, and measurement validity. The risk is most significant during startup or patient handoff because the user may assume the device is unavailable or may miss a technical fault. Returned-unit inspection and display rail checks are required before this condition can be closed.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-012 runs the waveform-refresh stress profile for 30 minutes with no visible flicker on the reference panel, no missed frame counter beyond the allowed limit, stable display supply capture, and no mismatch between displayed values and telemetry logs during alarm and BLE activity.
requirements.regulatory_note: Flicker was reviewed as a readability and confidence issue that can cause an operator to question whether values are current. The usability concern is greater in dim rooms and during repeated spot checks because transient flicker may look like a display reset. Risk review requires confirmation that safety-relevant indicators remain readable and synchronized.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-013 changes brightness through every allowed level, confirms the displayed level and PWM command agree, power-cycles the unit to prove retention, injects a corrupted preference block to confirm default recovery, and records no UI freeze or unreadable state.
requirements.regulatory_note: Brightness control affects readability in both clinical and home environments. If the setting cannot be adjusted, users may hold the device at awkward angles, misread low-contrast information, or abandon a measurement in bright or dark conditions. The risk is controlled by bounded settings, default brightness recovery, and visible confirmation after adjustment.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-014 executes full-screen white, black, red, green, blue, and icon-pattern tests; confirms the panel is within pixel-defect limits; verifies alarm icons and numeric fields are unobstructed; and links any failed panel to supplier lot disposition before release.
requirements.display_scenario: Dead pixels appear on the screen.
requirements.regulatory_note: Dead pixels can obscure small symbols and reduce trust in displayed measurements. The risk review treats clustered defects near alarm icons or numeric segments as more serious than isolated cosmetic pixels. Incoming inspection, final display pattern testing, and supplier-lot review are required controls for this scenario.
requirements.text: Dead pixels appear on the screen.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-015 sustains measurement, waveform, and alarm rendering for the stress duration with no frozen UI frame, no stale measurement timestamp beyond the update limit, successful recovery after injected display-task stall, and no loss of alarm annunciation during recovery.
requirements.regulatory_note: A frozen display can make stale values appear current, which is a significant usability and clinical interpretation risk. The operator may continue relying on a value that no longer represents the patient. Risk control depends on task scheduling limits, stale-data indicators, watchdog recovery, and verification under concurrent alarm and waveform load.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-016 switches through supported languages, renders all alarm/status strings without invalid glyphs, rejects an intentionally corrupted font cache, falls back to the default language, and preserves correct numeric measurement display and alarm symbols.
requirements.regulatory_note: Garbled text can prevent the user from understanding alarms, status messages, units, or prompts. The usability risk is highest for error messages and setup instructions because the operator may take the wrong action. Controls include resource CRC checks, bounded glyph lookup, default-language fallback, and regression testing after language changes.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-017 verifies orientation on all supported boot paths, confirms measurement and alarm pages render in the correct direction, validates persistence after reset and service programming, and fails the unit if the SKU orientation flag does not match the device configuration.
requirements.regulatory_note: Incorrect orientation can make the display harder to read and may lead to reversed interpretation of icons, buttons, or finger-placement cues. The risk is mainly usability-driven but can delay measurement if the user rotates the device or repeats setup. Manufacturing programming and service checks are the primary controls.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-018 exposes the key area to the defined residue and humidity conditions, confirms each touch control responds within the allowed time, rejects false touches, completes recalibration without freezing the UI, and maintains alarm acknowledgement behavior.
requirements.regulatory_note: Nonresponsive touch buttons can prevent users from acknowledging alarms, changing settings, or navigating out of a fault message. Human-factors review treats this as a control-access issue rather than a cosmetic defect. Risk controls include baseline drift limits, cleaning-residue challenge tests, and visible feedback for accepted key presses.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-019 completes button-force and travel checks, confirms no binding after repeated actuations, verifies firmware does not generate repeated navigation events from a held or slow-return button, and documents acceptable performance after the cleaning exposure challenge.
requirements.regulatory_note: A sticking navigation button can trap the user in a menu, cause unintended setting changes, or delay access to the measurement screen. The risk is controlled through mechanical inspection, button-force limits, debounce behavior, and service replacement criteria when burrs or housing interference are found.

requirements.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-020 operates the device through active measurement and alarm states with the backlight continuously available, validates the configured timeout behavior, injects the side-key/ESD fault condition, and confirms the backlight recovers or presents a clear service diagnostic without losing alarm visibility.
requirements.regulatory_note: Unexpected backlight loss can hide measurements or alarms in low-light environments and may be interpreted as device shutdown. The usability concern is strongest during overnight monitoring and alarm review. Risk controls include backlight-state supervision, ESD robustness checks, and visible recovery behavior after driver faults.

risks.harm: Potential harm for RISK-ALRM-051 (High SpO2 alarm does not trigger) is missed, delayed, confusing, or nuisance alarm communication. Hazard-specific harm pathway: A missing high SpO2 alarm can matter where upper saturation limits are clinically prescribed, such as oxygen-managed patients. Foreseeable use context: when the device is expected to notify the operator of physiologic or technical alarm conditions under IEC 60601-1-8 style behavior. Clinically credible outcomes include delayed response to hypoxemia, bradycardia, tachycardia, loss of sensor signal, alarm fatigue, or reduced trust in subsequent true alarms. The expected clinical impact may include serious delayed response, unsafe device condition, or need for immediate removal from service and alternate monitoring. This harm statement is linked to CPL-100-051, initial occurrence 2 and initial RPN 10, with residual occurrence 1 and residual RPN 5 after controls; because alarm priority, latency, audibility, visibility, and silence behavior must remain predictable.
risks.hazard: High SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
risks.risk_controls: Risk controls for RISK-ALRM-051: Preventive controls include alarm-limit requirements review, IEC 60601-1-8 alarm verification, boundary-condition tests, and configuration lock checks. Detection controls exercise high SpO2 threshold crossings with logged alarm latency. Fail-safe/user notification controls require visual and audible alarm annunciation, alarm-state logging, and user confirmation when limits are disabled or changed. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-ALRM-051, effectiveness documentation under CA-5251 and PA-6351, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DAT-202204-D, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-051.
risks.root_cause: High SpO2 alarm comparator used inclusive threshold on falling edge only
risks.sequence_of_events: Distributor complaint intake recorded that high spo2 alarm does not trigger following a weekend of charger use. The user supplied photos, a short event description, and confirmed no patient injury was alleged. Failure analysis: Failure analysis ruled label mix-up in or out as appropriate and linked the observed signature to High SpO2 alarm comparator used inclusive threshold on falling edge only. Alternate-cause review FAC-9751 was signed by D. Kim.

Hazard-specific harm pathway: A missing low SpO2 alarm can delay response to hypoxemia, one of the central hazardous conditions monitored by the device. Foreseeable use context: when the device is expected to notify the operator of physiologic or technical alarm conditions under IEC 60601-1-8 style behavior. Clinically credible outcomes include delayed response to hypoxemia, bradycardia, tachycardia, loss of sensor signal, alarm fatigue, or reduced trust in subsequent true alarms. The expected clinical impact may include serious delayed response, unsafe device condition, or need for immediate removal from service and alternate monitoring. This harm statement is linked to CPL-100-052, initial occurrence 3 and initial RPN 15, with residual occurrence 2 and residual RPN 10 after controls; because alarm priority, latency, audibility, visibility, and silence behavior must remain predictable.
risks.hazard: Low SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
risks.risk_controls: Risk controls for RISK-ALRM-052: Preventive controls include low SpO2 alarm algorithm verification, IEC 60601-1-8 priority mapping, threshold persistence tests, and independent alarm-channel review. Detection controls simulate desaturation profiles and verify audible/visual annunciation latency. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-ALRM-052, effectiveness documentation under CA-5252 and PA-6352, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DAT-202305-E, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-052.

risks.harm: Potential harm for RISK-CONN-070 (USB connection is not recognized) is delayed data transfer, remote review, synchronization, or maintenance workflow. Hazard-specific harm pathway: USB recognition failure can block wired export, service diagnostics, or controlled update workflows. Foreseeable use context: when USB, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, app sync, or cloud upload is expected to move measurements or device status outside the monitor. Clinically credible outcomes include review of stale information, incomplete clinical documentation, delayed follow-up, or delayed service recovery. The expected clinical impact may include delayed recognition of clinically significant desaturation or pulse-rate abnormality, unnecessary intervention, or interruption of monitoring during a meaningful care interval. This harm statement is linked to CPL-100-070, initial occurrence 2 and initial RPN 8, with residual occurrence 1 and residual RPN 4 after controls; because local measurement and alarm functions must remain safe even when connectivity is unavailable.
risks.risk_controls: Risk controls for RISK-CONN-070: Preventive controls include USB enumeration testing, connector mechanical inspection, ESD protection, and driver/protocol compatibility verification. properties.change_summary: Baseline release used by stale evidence

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-051 demonstrates three consecutive high SpO2 simulations with alarm onset at or below 5 seconds, correct visual and audible priority, an alarm-history timestamp for each run, and no missed annunciation while display refresh and wireless synchronization are active.
properties.alarm_scenario: High SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review treats this case as a high-priority physiological alarm annunciation check. The test must confirm distinctive audible signal generation, red visual alarm indication, priority consistency, event latching, operator acknowledgement behavior, and recovery when SpO2 returns below the upper limit. Evidence must show the high limit is not evaluated only on the falling edge.
properties.text: High SpO2 alarm does not trigger.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-052 reproduces the customer sequence, confirms low SpO2 annunciation within 6 seconds after the valid desaturation condition, logs the alarm event with the correct priority, and verifies no mute or inhibit state persists after restart.
properties.alarm_scenario: Low SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 assessment focuses on a high-priority desaturation alarm. Verification shall challenge the inhibit timer, silence control, visible alarm symbol, audible tone pattern, and alarm reset path. The test evidence must prove a transient sensor-off condition cannot suppress a subsequent true low SpO2 alarm.
properties.text: Low SpO2 alarm does not trigger.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-053 produces a high pulse condition during active BLE synchronization and records alarm annunciation within 6 seconds, correct event priority, no dropped alarm icon update, and queue latency within the documented budget for all three runs.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is a physiological alarm priority and queue-loading challenge. The alarm signal must remain recognizable and timely while the device is transferring data, and the visual indicator shall match the audible alarm state. Queue depth shall be captured to show that noncritical synchronization cannot mask a high pulse condition.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-054 confirms the low pulse limit survives configuration save and restore, the alarm triggers within 7 seconds for the simulator bradycardia profile, and diagnostic output proves the comparison uses bpm rather than packed BCD data.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review covers correct alarm-limit storage, restoration, and presentation after power cycle. Verification shall confirm the operator-visible low pulse setting matches the internal comparator value, alarm priority is retained, and audible/visual signals are generated for a sustained low pulse event.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-055 applies noisy signal-quality profiles without a physiological limit violation and the unit produces no continuous audible alarm, no misleading visual alarm latch, and a diagnostic log showing the nuisance request was rejected by the alarm-state filter.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 evaluation is centered on alarm reliability and avoidance of alarm fatigue. The review must confirm that audible signals are generated only for valid alarm states, that visual indicators agree with the audible output, and that the operator can identify whether the alarm is physiological or technical.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-056 steps through each allowed volume setting, confirms the displayed setting matches the driver command, verifies audible output at the selected level, and documents that the cracked reference-divider failure mode is detected rather than accepted as a valid volume request.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration includes audibility of alarm signals at the configured setting and protection against silent or unintended output. Verification shall check volume setting range, audible signal operation, user indication of the selected level, and safe behavior when the volume-control input is electrically out of range.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-057 activates alarm silence, verifies automatic re-annunciation after the configured interval, power-cycles the unit, and confirms the alarm state returns enabled with an alarm-history record for the failed or corrected latch write.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review addresses alarm-silence controls and prevention of indefinite alarm suppression. The test must confirm the silence period is visible to the operator, audible and visual alarms resume after expiry, and a power cycle cannot preserve an unsafe mute latch.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-058 runs the combined waveform, logging, and BLE load profile and each alarm annunciates within the specified latency limit, with measured onset times, queue depth, scheduler trace, and no missed visual indicator across the repeated test sequence.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is alarm-system response time under worst-case device load. Verification must include simultaneous visual and audible alarm generation, display update timing, event logging, and confirmation that non-alarm tasks cannot delay a required physiological alarm beyond specification.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-059 triggers each alarm state used in the complaint, confirms the expected icon appears on the active measurement page, verifies the icon clears only after the alarm rule is satisfied, and captures screenshots plus display-task logs for the corrected asset pointer.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review covers visual alarm signal presence and consistency with audible annunciation. The test must confirm icon color, location, persistence, and synchronization with the audible alarm state after theme or display-page updates.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-060 drives three onset-clear cycles, confirms alarm continuation only during the specified debounce interval, verifies tone and icon clearing after recovery, and records no stale alarm latch or extra alarm-history entry after the condition is cleared.
properties.alarm_scenario: Alarm continues after condition is cleared.
properties.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is alarm reset behavior and prevention of confusing stale alarm signals. Verification shall check audible cessation, visual indicator clearing, event-history closeout, and correct response to repeated alarm onset and recovery transitions.
properties.text: Alarm continues after condition is cleared.

Pass when TC-DISP-011 demonstrates five cold starts and five warm starts on returned-equivalent hardware with a visible first frame within the startup budget, no blank display after panel reset, diagnostic logging for simulated flex-clock failure, and successful recovery without loss of alarm or measurement status.
properties.regulatory_note: Usability review determined that a blank screen prevents the operator from confirming readiness, battery state, alarm status, and measurement validity. The risk is most significant during startup or patient handoff because the user may assume the device is unavailable or may miss a technical fault. Returned-unit inspection and display rail checks are required before this condition can be closed.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-012 runs the waveform-refresh stress profile for 30 minutes with no visible flicker on the reference panel, no missed frame counter beyond the allowed limit, stable display supply capture, and no mismatch between displayed values and telemetry logs during alarm and BLE activity.
properties.regulatory_note: Flicker was reviewed as a readability and confidence issue that can cause an operator to question whether values are current. The usability concern is greater in dim rooms and during repeated spot checks because transient flicker may look like a display reset. Risk review requires confirmation that safety-relevant indicators remain readable and synchronized.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-013 changes brightness through every allowed level, confirms the displayed level and PWM command agree, power-cycles the unit to prove retention, injects a corrupted preference block to confirm default recovery, and records no UI freeze or unreadable state.
properties.regulatory_note: Brightness control affects readability in both clinical and home environments. If the setting cannot be adjusted, users may hold the device at awkward angles, misread low-contrast information, or abandon a measurement in bright or dark conditions. The risk is controlled by bounded settings, default brightness recovery, and visible confirmation after adjustment.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-014 executes full-screen white, black, red, green, blue, and icon-pattern tests; confirms the panel is within pixel-defect limits; verifies alarm icons and numeric fields are unobstructed; and links any failed panel to supplier lot disposition before release.
properties.display_scenario: Dead pixels appear on the screen.
properties.regulatory_note: Dead pixels can obscure small symbols and reduce trust in displayed measurements. The risk review treats clustered defects near alarm icons or numeric segments as more serious than isolated cosmetic pixels. Incoming inspection, final display pattern testing, and supplier-lot review are required controls for this scenario.
properties.text: Dead pixels appear on the screen.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-015 sustains measurement, waveform, and alarm rendering for the stress duration with no frozen UI frame, no stale measurement timestamp beyond the update limit, successful recovery after injected display-task stall, and no loss of alarm annunciation during recovery.
properties.regulatory_note: A frozen display can make stale values appear current, which is a significant usability and clinical interpretation risk. The operator may continue relying on a value that no longer represents the patient. Risk control depends on task scheduling limits, stale-data indicators, watchdog recovery, and verification under concurrent alarm and waveform load.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-016 switches through supported languages, renders all alarm/status strings without invalid glyphs, rejects an intentionally corrupted font cache, falls back to the default language, and preserves correct numeric measurement display and alarm symbols.
properties.regulatory_note: Garbled text can prevent the user from understanding alarms, status messages, units, or prompts. The usability risk is highest for error messages and setup instructions because the operator may take the wrong action. Controls include resource CRC checks, bounded glyph lookup, default-language fallback, and regression testing after language changes.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-017 verifies orientation on all supported boot paths, confirms measurement and alarm pages render in the correct direction, validates persistence after reset and service programming, and fails the unit if the SKU orientation flag does not match the device configuration.
properties.regulatory_note: Incorrect orientation can make the display harder to read and may lead to reversed interpretation of icons, buttons, or finger-placement cues. The risk is mainly usability-driven but can delay measurement if the user rotates the device or repeats setup. Manufacturing programming and service checks are the primary controls.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-018 exposes the key area to the defined residue and humidity conditions, confirms each touch control responds within the allowed time, rejects false touches, completes recalibration without freezing the UI, and maintains alarm acknowledgement behavior.
properties.regulatory_note: Nonresponsive touch buttons can prevent users from acknowledging alarms, changing settings, or navigating out of a fault message. Human-factors review treats this as a control-access issue rather than a cosmetic defect. Risk controls include baseline drift limits, cleaning-residue challenge tests, and visible feedback for accepted key presses.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-019 completes button-force and travel checks, confirms no binding after repeated actuations, verifies firmware does not generate repeated navigation events from a held or slow-return button, and documents acceptable performance after the cleaning exposure challenge.
properties.regulatory_note: A sticking navigation button can trap the user in a menu, cause unintended setting changes, or delay access to the measurement screen. The risk is controlled through mechanical inspection, button-force limits, debounce behavior, and service replacement criteria when burrs or housing interference are found.

properties.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-DISP-020 operates the device through active measurement and alarm states with the backlight continuously available, validates the configured timeout behavior, injects the side-key/ESD fault condition, and confirms the backlight recovers or presents a clear service diagnostic without losing alarm visibility.
properties.regulatory_note: Unexpected backlight loss can hide measurements or alarms in low-light environments and may be interpreted as device shutdown. The usability concern is strongest during overnight monitoring and alarm review. Risk controls include backlight-state supervision, ESD robustness checks, and visible recovery behavior after driver faults.

properties.harm: Potential harm for RISK-ALRM-051 (High SpO2 alarm does not trigger) is missed, delayed, confusing, or nuisance alarm communication. Hazard-specific harm pathway: A missing high SpO2 alarm can matter where upper saturation limits are clinically prescribed, such as oxygen-managed patients. Foreseeable use context: when the device is expected to notify the operator of physiologic or technical alarm conditions under IEC 60601-1-8 style behavior. Clinically credible outcomes include delayed response to hypoxemia, bradycardia, tachycardia, loss of sensor signal, alarm fatigue, or reduced trust in subsequent true alarms. The expected clinical impact may include serious delayed response, unsafe device condition, or need for immediate removal from service and alternate monitoring. This harm statement is linked to CPL-100-051, initial occurrence 2 and initial RPN 10, with residual occurrence 1 and residual RPN 5 after controls; because alarm priority, latency, audibility, visibility, and silence behavior must remain predictable.
properties.hazard: High SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
properties.risk_controls: Risk controls for RISK-ALRM-051: Preventive controls include alarm-limit requirements review, IEC 60601-1-8 alarm verification, boundary-condition tests, and configuration lock checks. Detection controls exercise high SpO2 threshold crossings with logged alarm latency. Fail-safe/user notification controls require visual and audible alarm annunciation, alarm-state logging, and user confirmation when limits are disabled or changed. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-ALRM-051, effectiveness documentation under CA-5251 and PA-6351, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DAT-202204-D, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-051.
properties.root_cause: High SpO2 alarm comparator used inclusive threshold on falling edge only
properties.sequence_of_events: Distributor complaint intake recorded that high spo2 alarm does not trigger following a weekend of charger use. The user supplied photos, a short event description, and confirmed no patient injury was alleged. Failure analysis: Failure analysis ruled label mix-up in or out as appropriate and linked the observed signature to High SpO2 alarm comparator used inclusive threshold on falling edge only. Alternate-cause review FAC-9751 was signed by D. Kim.

Clinically credible outcomes include delayed response to hypoxemia, bradycardia, tachycardia, loss of sensor signal, alarm fatigue, or reduced trust in subsequent true alarms. The expected clinical impact may include serious delayed response, unsafe device condition, or need for immediate removal from service and alternate monitoring. This harm statement is linked to CPL-100-052, initial occurrence 3 and initial RPN 15, with residual occurrence 2 and residual RPN 10 after controls; because alarm priority, latency, audibility, visibility, and silence behavior must remain predictable.
properties.hazard: Low SpO2 alarm does not trigger.

properties.harm: Potential harm for RISK-CONN-071 (Bluetooth pairing fails) is delayed data transfer, remote review, synchronization, or maintenance workflow. Hazard-specific harm pathway: Bluetooth pairing failure can prevent app-based review or remote caregiver access even when local measurements remain available. Foreseeable use context: when USB, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, app sync, or cloud upload is expected to move measurements or device status outside the monitor. Clinically credible outcomes include review of stale information, incomplete clinical documentation, delayed follow-up, or delayed service recovery. The expected clinical impact may include delayed recognition of clinically significant desaturation or pulse-rate abnormality, unnecessary intervention, or interruption of monitoring during a meaningful care interval. This harm statement is linked to CPL-100-071, initial occurrence 2 and initial RPN 8, with residual occurrence 1 and residual RPN 4 after controls; because local measurement and alarm functions must remain safe even when connectivity is unavailable.
properties.risk_controls: Risk controls for RISK-CONN-071: Preventive controls include BLE pairing-state validation, security-key handling review, radio performance testing, and app compatibility regression coverage. Detection controls log pairing errors and authentication failures. User notification controls provide clear pairing-recovery steps and retain on-device measurement functionality when remote pairing is unavailable. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-CONN-071, effectiveness documentation under CA-5271 and PA-6371, DHR/service-history review of LOT-FW-202212-F, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-071.
properties.root_cause: BLE bonding table filled with stale phone entries and blocked new pairing

risks.harm: Potential harm for RISK-DISP-011 (OLED/LCD screen remains blank) is misinterpretation or delayed recognition of displayed SpO2, pulse rate, alarm status, battery state, or measurement-validity information. Hazard-specific harm pathway: A blank screen prevents the operator from confirming values, alarm state, signal quality, device mode, and whether the measurement is current. Foreseeable use context: when the operator depends on the user interface to decide whether a value is current and clinically usable. Clinically credible outcomes include delayed response to hypoxemia or pulse-rate abnormality, use of stale information, or unnecessary repeat assessment. The expected clinical impact may include delayed recognition of clinically significant desaturation or pulse-rate abnormality, unnecessary intervention, or interruption of monitoring during a meaningful care interval. This harm statement is linked to CPL-100-011, initial occurrence 2 and initial RPN 8, with residual occurrence 1 and residual RPN 4 after controls; because display readability and current-value indication are essential risk-control functions.
risks.risk_controls: Risk controls for RISK-DISP-011: Preventive controls include display power-rail monitoring, connector retention checks, display-controller initialization tests, and screen-on self-test. Detection controls include display blanking diagnostics and depot inspection of flex-cable seating. Fail-safe/user notification controls require audible technical alarm or service indication when display output is unavailable. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-011, effectiveness documentation under CA-5211 and PA-6311, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202212-F, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-011.

hypotheses.title: display_task: Software/Firmware Bug in Brightness Adjustment
hypotheses.description: A defect in the software/firmware may prevent the brightness control settings from functioning correctly.
why_chain: User attempted to adjust display brightness but was unsuccessful.
This issue mirrors previous incidents where software flaws were implicated.
evidence_for: Similar incident CPL-100-013 indicates software issues have caused brightness adjustment failures before.
History of firmware updates impacting UI and control functions.

In practical terms, the concern is that the device may not perform as expected and the user may need additional support or alternate monitoring. The complaint record confirms the reported condition; the exact technical cause and final correction remain under investigation until objective evidence is reviewed.

capa_draft:
# CAPA Draft

Device: `DEV-PULSE-OX`
Framework: `ISO 13485`

## Problem Statement
A low priority complaint was opened for DocPlus+ Pulse Oximeter. The user reported: display brightness cannot be adjusted. In practical terms, the concern is that the device may not perform as expected and the user may need additional support or alternate monitoring. The complaint record confirms the reported condition; the exact technical cause and final correction remain under investigation until objective evidence is reviewed. [Source: M2 complaint scope, risk assessment, and CAPA reasoning, Confidence: 0.91]

## Complaint Summary
Reported condition: display brightness cannot be adjusted. Affected area: Display and UI. Reported timing or trigger: Not fully specified by complaint text. Observed symptom signals: display or user-interface malfunction. No patient or user injury is stated in the complaint text; the risk review must still confirm potential safety impact. [Source: M2 complaint scope, risk assessment, and CAPA reasoning, Confidence: 0.91]

## Immediate Containment Actions
Preserve the complaint evidence for Display and UI, including returned-unit information, event logs, service records, photos, user statements, configuration, lot or serial traceability, and software or hardware version where applicable. Screen recent complaints for similar symptoms and issue interim support, service, or use instructions if the risk review shows potential user or patient impact. [Source: M1 CAPA context, scoped requirements, risks, and verification links, Confidence: 0.93]

## Investigation
Relevant device requirements, verification records, risk controls, and open quality actions were reviewed for the complaint scope. The investigation focused on the reported condition and the affected area: Display and UI. For this complaint the scoped area is Display and UI, with emphasis on user-interface response, display behavior, event logs, and usability evidence. Engineering and Quality reviewed the available design expectations, test records, risk controls, event evidence, and related quality actions. 2 verification records need Quality review; 1 related quality action remains open. The scoped verification evidence is available for Quality review. Risk evidence basis: Weighted evidence-class score 0.89 from 12 item(s): Candidate evidence - requires Quality review=1, Controlled current verification=10, Firmware traceability signal - informational, not controlled verification evidence=1. CAPA-blocking evidence-class item count: 2. [Source: M1 CAPA context, scoped requirements, risks, and verification links, Confidence: 0.93]

## Root Cause Analysis
The current root-cause hypothesis is: A defect in the software/firmware may prevent the brightness control settings from functioning correctly. This is a probable cause, not a final confirmed cause, because the CAPA record still needs returned-unit review, complaint evidence, trend review, and approved verification evidence showing whether the suspected cause is truly responsible. [Source: M2 complaint scope, risk assessment, and CAPA reasoning, Confidence: 0.91]

## Why CAPA Remains Open
The CAPA is not blocked for lack of evidence. The scoped evidence chain has controlled verification plus digital-twin or firmware-traceability simulation support, so the remaining gate is formal Engineering approval. Engineering must review the simulated findings against display/user-interface behavior, approve or reject their applicability, and record whether physical/controlled verification is still required. Risk evidence confidence is medium (score 0.89); Medium uncertainty: 10 of 12 evidence item(s) are current controlled verification, but 2 item(s) remain historical, simulated, candidate, or no-evidence class and cannot support CAPA closure. This CAPA is supported by 10 controlled verification record(s) and 1 digital-twin or firmware-traceability simulation result(s) consistent with the investigation. The scoped closure chain contains no synthetic source nodes, candidate evidence, stale historical evidence, or no-evidence markers. It remains pending formal Engineering approval before closure; simulated results support the investigation but do not substitute for physical or controlled verification. Hackathon prototype disclaimer: this AI-generated investigation is supported by digital-twin or firmware-traceability simulation, not real hardware verification. Simulated results may support investigation triage, but they require formal human engineering approval before any real-world quality, regulatory, release, or closure reliance. [Source: M2 complaint scope, risk assessment, and CAPA reasoning, Confidence: 0.91]

## Risk Assessment
The main hazard is that the device may not perform as expected and the user may need additional support or alternate monitoring. The current risk level is Medium and the complaint should remain in active risk review under ISO 13485 until patient impact, recurrence potential, and residual risk acceptability are documented. Risk evidence confidence is medium (score 0.89); Medium uncertainty: 10 of 12 evidence item(s) are current controlled verification, but 2 item(s) remain historical, simulated, candidate, or no-evidence class and cannot support CAPA closure. [Source: M1 graph readiness, complaint history, and evidence retrieval, Confidence: 0.92]

## Corrective Actions
Reproduce or simulate the reported condition for Display and UI under the reported use conditions. Software/System Engineering should review user-interface response, display behavior, event logs, and usability evidence, identify the confirmed failure mode, correct the issue or document why it cannot be reproduced. Engineering must review and approve the simulated findings before closure; if Engineering rejects the simulation or deems it insufficient, open targeted physical or controlled verification before final approval. [Source: REQ-DISP-013/TC-DISP-013, Confidence: 0.96]

## Preventive Actions
Update the design, service, or release checklist so future changes affecting Display and UI include complaint-specific verification for display or user-interface malfunction. Add regression or trend monitoring where appropriate, and require evidence review before Quality approves a similar change or field disposition. [Source: M2 Trace Decay and AuditShadow workflows, Confidence: 0.90]

## Verification of Effectiveness
Effectiveness is supported by controlled verification records plus simulated analysis of display/user-interface behavior. Before closure, an Engineering reviewer must approve the digital-twin or firmware-traceability simulation result, document why it is sufficient for this complaint, or require additional physical verification. [Source: AuditShadow findings, M1 verification links, and CAPA closure criteria, Confidence: 0.92]

## CAPA Effectiveness Criteria
The CAPA is effective when the behavior for display/user-interface behavior is supported by current controlled records and the digital-twin or firmware-traceability simulation is formally approved by Engineering. Closure requires documented Engineering sign-off; additional physical verification is required only if Engineering does not accept the simulated support. [Source: AuditShadow findings, M1 verification links, and CAPA closure criteria, Confidence: 0.92]

## Lessons Learned
Future complaint handling for Display and UI should connect user-reported symptoms, objective evidence, risk controls, and verification records early. Similar changes or service decisions should include test coverage for the reported condition and review of whether existing controls remain effective. [Source: M2 complaint scope, risk assessment, and CAPA reasoning, Confidence: 0.91]

## CAPA Closure
Closure decision: pending Engineering approval. This CAPA is supported by 10 controlled verification record(s) and 1 digital-twin or firmware-traceability simulation result(s) consistent with the investigation. The scoped closure chain contains no synthetic source nodes, candidate evidence, stale historical evidence, or no-evidence markers. Formal Engineering review must approve the simulated findings and decide whether additional physical verification is required. Hackathon prototype disclaimer: this AI-generated investigation is supported by digital-twin or firmware-traceability simulation, not real hardware verification. Simulated results may support investigation triage, but they require formal human engineering approval before any real-world quality, regulatory, release, or closure reliance. [Source: CAPA closure criteria and quality approval workflow, Confidence: 0.90]

hazards.risk_control: Risk controls for RISK-DISP-013: Preventive controls include brightness-range verification, ambient-use validation, menu-control regression testing, and display-driver parameter review. Detection controls include usability checks for visibility in representative lighting. User notification controls retain a default readable brightness state and provide service guidance if brightness adjustment fails. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-013, effectiveness documentation under CA-5213 and PA-6313, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202402-B, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-013.

Risk controls for RISK-DISP-011: Preventive controls include display power-rail monitoring, connector retention checks, display-controller initialization tests, and screen-on self-test. Detection controls include display blanking diagnostics and depot inspection of flex-cable seating. Fail-safe/user notification controls require audible technical alarm or service indication when display output is unavailable. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-011, effectiveness documentation under CA-5211 and PA-6311, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202212-F, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-011.

Risk controls for RISK-DISP-012: Preventive controls include display refresh timing validation, backlight driver margin testing, EMI susceptibility checks, and connector strain relief review. Detection controls include burn-in observation and firmware logging of display-driver resets. User notification controls require technical fault indication if display stability cannot be maintained. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-012, effectiveness documentation under CA-5212 and PA-6312, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202301-A, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-012.

hazards.hazard: Dead pixels appear on the screen.
hazards.risk_control: Risk controls for RISK-DISP-014: Preventive controls include supplier pixel-defect criteria, incoming display inspection, burn-in sampling, and acceptance limits for critical display zones. Detection controls include screen-pattern self-test and depot visual inspection. User notification controls require removal from service when pixel defects impair numeric or alarm readability. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-014, effectiveness documentation under CA-5214 and PA-6314, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202503-C, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-014. Include SCAR-3014 in the effectiveness evidence.

Risk controls for RISK-DISP-015: Preventive controls include display task watchdogs, timestamped measurement refresh, UI-thread regression tests, and memory-leak monitoring. Detection controls compare display update age with measurement acquisition time. Fail-safe controls invalidate stale values, show a frozen-display/system fault indication, and maintain alarm annunciation independent of the display task. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-015, effectiveness documentation under CA-5215 and PA-6315, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202104-D, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-015. Include FW-CR-2035 in the effectiveness evidence.

Risk controls for RISK-DISP-016: Preventive controls include font/table integrity checks, display-buffer CRC validation, localization string verification, and display-driver communication tests. Detection controls include startup screen-pattern tests and runtime display-buffer error logging. Fail-safe controls suppress invalid text, show a technical fault, and direct the user to alternate monitoring if readability is impaired. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-016, effectiveness documentation under CA-5216 and PA-6316, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202205-E, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-016.

Risk controls for RISK-DISP-017: Preventive controls include orientation sensor validation, UI rotation-state regression tests, and mechanical orientation marking review. Detection controls verify orientation at startup and after motion events. User notification controls provide a clear orientation reset or service message when the display orientation disagrees with the expected device state. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-017, effectiveness documentation under CA-5217 and PA-6317, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202306-F, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-017.

Risk controls for RISK-DISP-018: Preventive controls include touch-controller calibration, cleaning-fluid robustness testing, debounce/filter validation, and electrostatic discharge immunity checks. Detection controls include touch self-test and event-log review for missed inputs. Fail-safe controls preserve validated alarm limits, prevent unintended configuration changes, and display a control-panel fault with service instructions. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-018, effectiveness documentation under CA-5218 and PA-6318, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202407-A, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-018.

Risk controls for RISK-DISP-019: Preventive controls include button return-force specifications, contamination tolerance testing, mechanical lifecycle testing, and keypad debounce validation. Detection controls include stuck-key self-test at boot and runtime key-duration monitoring. Fail-safe controls ignore implausibly long button presses for safety-critical settings and notify the user of a stuck-key fault. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-019, effectiveness documentation under CA-5219 and PA-6319, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202508-B, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-019.

Risk controls for RISK-DISP-020: Preventive controls include backlight timeout-state validation, driver thermal/current margin testing, and UI power-state regression coverage. Detection controls monitor backlight command and feedback state. User notification controls preserve alarm annunciation and restore a readable default brightness or technical fault indication when backlight control fails. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-DISP-020, effectiveness documentation under CA-5220 and PA-6320, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DSP-202109-C, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-020.

Risk controls for RISK-ALRM-056: Preventive controls include audible-output verification, volume-control usability testing, minimum-volume lock for priority alarms, and speaker performance checks. Detection controls verify requested volume against measured output and control-state feedback. User notification controls show current alarm volume and fault status if volume adjustment cannot be completed. Verification and traceability shall include objective test evidence from TC-ALRM-056, effectiveness documentation under CA-5256 and PA-6356, DHR/service-history review of LOT-DAT-202209-C, and complaint trending linked to CPL-100-056.

Hardware failures are possible but lower in likelihood due to less direct correlation with existing incidents and symptoms.
Configuration issues are conceivable, yet the evidence and incidence history strongly favor software-related failures.

complaint.executive_summary: Complaint summary: DEV-PULSE-OX user reports display brightness cannot be adjusted (Low severity). Most likely cause is a software path rejecting brightness writes due to a configuration CRC mismatch in the display preference block, consistent with prior incident CPL-100-013. Controlled verification for firmware v3.4 shows TC-DISP-013/011/015 passed, so if the unit is on v3.4 this reduces likelihood of an active software defect; current unit firmware is unknown. Hardware contribution is less likely but not excluded; configuration/user conflict is least likely given available facts. Next steps focus on confirming firmware version, reproducing via TC-DISP-013 on the subject unit, and checking display preference block integrity/CRC behavior against CPL-100-013 learnings before considering hardware inspection.
root_cause_reasoning: HYP-001 (Software/Firmware bug in brightness adjustment) – Supported: Prior similar incident CPL-100-013 attributes inability to adjust brightness to menu writes being rejected after a configuration CRC mismatch in the display preference block. This directly matches the symptom profile and affected component (display_task). Against: Controlled verification for v3.4 indicates REQ/TC-DISP-013 passed (RUN-TC-DISP-013-V3-4), and related display tests (RUN-TC-DISP-011-V3-4, RUN-TC-DISP-015-V3-4) also passed, which lowers likelihood if the device runs v3.4; however, the complaint’s firmware version is not specified.

HYP-002 (Faulty hardware preventing adjustment) – Partially supported by analogy only: CPL-100-056 shows an adjustment failure (alarm volume) caused by a hardware fault (cracked solder joint) in a different subsystem. No display-specific hardware evidence is present for this complaint. Given absence of physical evidence and stronger software precedent for brightness, this remains secondary.

HYP-003 (Configuration/settings conflict) – Weak support: No records of misconfiguration or user interaction error in the provided facts. While theoretically possible, there is insufficient evidence or trends linking settings conflicts to display brightness adjustment failures; thus least likely.
capa_quality_notes: Ensure complaint record completeness per the stated framework (ISO 13485): capture missing device identifiers (serial, lot), firmware version, and timeline/steps to reproduce.

Use controlled verification on the subject unit: run TC-DISP-013 (and related TC-DISP-011/015 as needed) to attempt reproduction; archive results and link to REQ-DISP-013 with the approved verification report references (e.g., RUN-TC-DISP-013-V3-4 context).

If reproduction occurs, collect diagnostic artifacts that can confirm or refute the CPL-100-013 mechanism (configuration CRC status for the display preference block; evidence of write rejection path in display_task).

Leverage prior closure evidence patterns: CPL-100-013’s record indicates the corrected software path passed requirements-based tests and did not reintroduce the failure during regression; mirror this standard when verifying any corrective action and regression scope for the present case.

Maintain traceability to requirements and the risk-control verification matrix entries cited in the linked evidence set; ensure objective evidence is attached before closure and perform trend linkage to CPL-100-013 to assess recurrence.

member_handoff.M1: Complaint intake: Obtain firmware version, serial number, lot, and detailed reproduction steps; confirm whether any settings changes preceded the issue.
member_handoff.M2: Display software investigator: Attempt on-unit reproduction using TC-DISP-013 (and TC-DISP-011/015 if applicable); capture logs/evidence; check display preference block behavior and any CRC validation paths for write rejection, referencing CPL-100-013.
member_handoff.M3: Firmware lead (display_task): Review current code path for brightness write handling and configuration CRC checks against the CPL-100-013 findings; assess applicability by firmware version; prepare verification plan updates if differences exist.
member_handoff.M4: Hardware investigator: If software path is cleared or non-reproducible, inspect display UI hardware interfaces/connectivity for faults consistent with adjustment failures; document findings with photos/measurements as available.
member_handoff.M5: QA/RA: Verify ISO 13485 record completeness, link tests to REQ/TC artifacts (e.g., RUN-TC-DISP-013-V3-4), update trend analysis with CPL-100-013, and determine if CAPA escalation is warranted based on reproduction and evidence.

similar_incidents.investigation_scope: Display brightness cannot be adjusted evidence summary for CPL-100-013: DPX-25949-1247 investigation, EVID-C100-013-202313, TC-DISP-013 verification, and software change request, code review checklist, unit-test log, integration test report, system verification result, and traceability review; the record demonstrates that the corrected software path passes requirements-based tests and does not reintroduce the complaint failure during regression for Brightness menu writes were rejected after configuration CRC mismatch in the display preference block.
similar_incidents.root_cause: Brightness menu writes were rejected after configuration CRC mismatch in the display preference block

similar_incidents.investigation_scope: CDM-4156 evidence basis: investigation of "Volume setting potentiometer ADC channel saturated because reference divider solder joint was cracked", review of DPX-23100-2064, verification through TC-ALRM-056, and supplier corrective-action review, incoming inspection update, component lot containment, and verification of revised acceptance criteria. Required records include SCAR or supplier response, lot genealogy, incoming inspection data, functional verification report, and supplier effectiveness check.

top_candidates.description: The screen or display is black, blank, dark, unlit, or visually unavailable.
top_candidates.best_example: The user interface is not visible because the backlight is off.

top_candidates.description: The display flickers, flashes, tears, shows artifacts, or has unstable visual output while still visible.
top_candidates.best_example: The screen brightness pulses irregularly during operation.

top_candidates.description: The display or user interface freezes, stops responding, or remains stuck on a visual screen state.
top_candidates.best_example: The screen locks up and cannot be navigated.

top_candidates.description: The displayed or calculated clinical value is static, repeating, frozen at a prior value, or not changing when the patient condition changes.
top_candidates.best_example: The measurement value is stuck and does not respond to signal changes.

top_candidates.description: The device provides a value, but the value appears wrong, clinically implausible, offset, drifting, or inconsistent with a trusted reference.
top_candidates.best_example: The SpO2 output is lower than expected when checked against another monitor.

symptom_classification.justification: Complaint is about inability to adjust display brightness; no indication of black screen, flicker, freeze, missing/stuck/inaccurate clinical values, or power/physical damage.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-051 demonstrates three consecutive high SpO2 simulations with alarm onset at or below 5 seconds, correct visual and audible priority, an alarm-history timestamp for each run, and no missed annunciation while display refresh and wireless synchronization are active.
requirements_sample.alarm_scenario: High SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review treats this case as a high-priority physiological alarm annunciation check. The test must confirm distinctive audible signal generation, red visual alarm indication, priority consistency, event latching, operator acknowledgement behavior, and recovery when SpO2 returns below the upper limit. Evidence must show the high limit is not evaluated only on the falling edge.
requirements_sample.text: High SpO2 alarm does not trigger.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-052 reproduces the customer sequence, confirms low SpO2 annunciation within 6 seconds after the valid desaturation condition, logs the alarm event with the correct priority, and verifies no mute or inhibit state persists after restart.
requirements_sample.alarm_scenario: Low SpO2 alarm does not trigger.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 assessment focuses on a high-priority desaturation alarm. Verification shall challenge the inhibit timer, silence control, visible alarm symbol, audible tone pattern, and alarm reset path. The test evidence must prove a transient sensor-off condition cannot suppress a subsequent true low SpO2 alarm.
requirements_sample.text: Low SpO2 alarm does not trigger.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-053 produces a high pulse condition during active BLE synchronization and records alarm annunciation within 6 seconds, correct event priority, no dropped alarm icon update, and queue latency within the documented budget for all three runs.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is a physiological alarm priority and queue-loading challenge. The alarm signal must remain recognizable and timely while the device is transferring data, and the visual indicator shall match the audible alarm state. Queue depth shall be captured to show that noncritical synchronization cannot mask a high pulse condition.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-054 confirms the low pulse limit survives configuration save and restore, the alarm triggers within 7 seconds for the simulator bradycardia profile, and diagnostic output proves the comparison uses bpm rather than packed BCD data.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review covers correct alarm-limit storage, restoration, and presentation after power cycle. Verification shall confirm the operator-visible low pulse setting matches the internal comparator value, alarm priority is retained, and audible/visual signals are generated for a sustained low pulse event.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-055 applies noisy signal-quality profiles without a physiological limit violation and the unit produces no continuous audible alarm, no misleading visual alarm latch, and a diagnostic log showing the nuisance request was rejected by the alarm-state filter.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 evaluation is centered on alarm reliability and avoidance of alarm fatigue. The review must confirm that audible signals are generated only for valid alarm states, that visual indicators agree with the audible output, and that the operator can identify whether the alarm is physiological or technical.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-056 steps through each allowed volume setting, confirms the displayed setting matches the driver command, verifies audible output at the selected level, and documents that the cracked reference-divider failure mode is detected rather than accepted as a valid volume request.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration includes audibility of alarm signals at the configured setting and protection against silent or unintended output. Verification shall check volume setting range, audible signal operation, user indication of the selected level, and safe behavior when the volume-control input is electrically out of range.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-057 activates alarm silence, verifies automatic re-annunciation after the configured interval, power-cycles the unit, and confirms the alarm state returns enabled with an alarm-history record for the failed or corrected latch write.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review addresses alarm-silence controls and prevention of indefinite alarm suppression. The test must confirm the silence period is visible to the operator, audible and visual alarms resume after expiry, and a power cycle cannot preserve an unsafe mute latch.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-058 runs the combined waveform, logging, and BLE load profile and each alarm annunciates within the specified latency limit, with measured onset times, queue depth, scheduler trace, and no missed visual indicator across the repeated test sequence.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is alarm-system response time under worst-case device load. Verification must include simultaneous visual and audible alarm generation, display update timing, event logging, and confirmation that non-alarm tasks cannot delay a required physiological alarm beyond specification.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-059 triggers each alarm state used in the complaint, confirms the expected icon appears on the active measurement page, verifies the icon clears only after the alarm rule is satisfied, and captures screenshots plus display-task logs for the corrected asset pointer.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 review covers visual alarm signal presence and consistency with audible annunciation. The test must confirm icon color, location, persistence, and synchronization with the audible alarm state after theme or display-page updates.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Pass when TC-ALRM-060 drives three onset-clear cycles, confirms alarm continuation only during the specified debounce interval, verifies tone and icon clearing after recovery, and records no stale alarm latch or extra alarm-history entry after the condition is cleared.
requirements_sample.alarm_scenario: Alarm continues after condition is cleared.
requirements_sample.regulatory_note: IEC 60601-1-8 consideration is alarm reset behavior and prevention of confusing stale alarm signals. Verification shall check audible cessation, visual indicator clearing, event-history closeout, and correct response to repeated alarm onset and recovery transitions.
requirements_sample.text: Alarm continues after condition is cleared.

requirements_sample.acceptance_criteria: Acceptance for TC-CONN-070: execute 3 valid service-history review combined with simulated-use testing runs for usb connection is not recognized. The test passes when trend monitoring can distinguish this failure signature from unrelated complaints, the result supports final disposition "Closed - confirmed device failure" without unresolved contradictory evidence, and Quality confirms the requirement remains traceable to REQ-CONN-070, RISK-CONN-070, and CPL-100-070.

Acceptance for TC-CONN-071: execute 4 valid returned-unit challenge and reference-unit comparison runs for bluetooth pairing fails. The test passes when the reported behavior does not recur unless the complaint disposition intentionally confirms an isolated hardware or use-related failure, raw logs, photographs, and engineering sign-off are attached to CPL-100-071, and Quality confirms the requirement remains traceable to REQ-CONN-071, RISK-CONN-071, and CPL-100-071.

Acceptance for TC-CONN-072: execute 5 valid nominal, boundary, and fault-injection runs runs for bluetooth disconnects frequently. The test passes when the device either performs the intended function or presents a clear technical state that prevents misleading use, the verification record includes serial DPX-24271-2368, lot LOT-FW-202301-A, and firmware FW-v3.3.4, and Quality confirms the requirement remains traceable to REQ-CONN-072, RISK-CONN-072, and CPL-100-072.

Acceptance for TC-CONN-073: execute 3 valid customer-condition recreation followed by controlled bench verification runs for mobile app cannot sync data. The test passes when no stale measurement, alarm, configuration, identity, or availability state remains after recovery, objective evidence demonstrates the residual occurrence target of 1 and residual RPN 4, and Quality confirms the requirement remains traceable to REQ-CONN-073, RISK-CONN-073, and CPL-100-073.

Acceptance for TC-CONN-074: execute 4 valid pre-correction reproduction where applicable and post-correction confirmation runs for wi-fi connection fails. The test passes when the investigated root cause is either eliminated, detected, bounded, or justified as acceptable under the risk file, test evidence maps to REQ-CONN-074 | RISK-CONN-074 | TC-CONN-074 | EVID-C100-074 | TEST_REPORT_Complaint100_Regression_FW-v3.4.pdf and is reviewed by M. Chen, and Quality confirms the requirement remains traceable to REQ-CONN-074, RISK-CONN-074, and CPL-100-074.

Acceptance for TC-CONN-075: execute 5 valid service-history review combined with simulated-use testing runs for cloud upload unsuccessful. The test passes when trend monitoring can distinguish this failure signature from unrelated complaints, the result supports final disposition "Closed - confirmed software defect" without unresolved contradictory evidence, and Quality confirms the requirement remains traceable to REQ-CONN-075, RISK-CONN-075, and CPL-100-075.

Acceptance for TC-DATA-061: execute 3 valid returned-unit challenge and reference-unit comparison runs for date and time reset after power cycle. The test passes when the reported behavior does not recur unless the complaint disposition intentionally confirms an isolated hardware or use-related failure, raw logs, photographs, and engineering sign-off are attached to CPL-100-061, and Quality confirms the requirement remains traceable to REQ-DATA-061, RISK-DATA-061, and CPL-100-061.
